coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of high-performance carbon dots from laurel leaves and their application in anti-counterfeit ink
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Xiang
Wang, Jiayang
Ma, Yanshun
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: Green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) with high quantum yield were successfully synthesized through a one-step bottom-up approach, utilizing laurel leaves as the carbon precursor, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the nitrogen source, and N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC) as the solvent. Structural characterizations unveiled the presence of abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of G-CDs, significantly influencing their luminescent properties. The excitation and emission wavelengths of G-CDs were determined to be 467 nm and 508 nm, respectively, with a quantum yield of up to 49.9 %. Moreover, the emissions of G-CDs were found to be dependent on the excitation wavelength, suggesting a potential correlation between luminescence and particle size. Notably, the stability assessment demonstrated excellent salt resistance and strong photostability of the G-CDs. A brief discussion on the practical application of G-CDs in anti-counterfeit fluorescent ink highlighted their potential in this filed. This study presents a green synthesis strategy for producing high-performance carbon dots, reducing reliance on chemical reagents, lowering synthesis costs, and mitigating environmental pollution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Photoluminescence
Laurel leaves
Solvothermal method

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Estimation of relative fluorescence quantum yield

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of G-CDs. (a) TEM image. (b) HRTEM image, (c) Particle size distribution. (d) FT-IR spectrum. (e) XPS survey spectrum. (f-h) High resolution C1s, O1s and N1s spectra, respectively.

Optical features of G-CDs. (a) three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum. (b) two-dimensional projection. (c) UV-Vis absorption spectrum (purple), excitation spectrum (blue), emission spectrum (multi-color).
Excitation wavelength dependent emissions. (a) Emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths. (b) Normalized emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths.
Calculation of QY. (a) The relationship between integral fluorescence intensity and absorbance. (b) Comparison of QY of G-CDs in different solvents with quinine sulfate as standard.

Chemical stability. (a) Fluorescence spectra, (b) fluorescence intensities, (c) maximum emission wavelengths of G-CDs in different solvents. (d) The time-dependent fluorescence intensity. (e) The NaCl concentration-dependent fluorescence intensity.
Application of G-CDs solution as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink. (a) Handwriting is invisible in sunlight, (b) visible under UV light.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of high-performance carbon dots from laurel leaves and their application in anti-counterfeit ink

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Resources and Environment, Chengdu University of Information Technology, Chengdu 610041, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Resources and Environment, Chengdu University of Information Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Resources and Environment, Chengdu University of Information Technology, Chengdu 610041, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) with high quantum yield were successfully synthesized through a one-step bottom-up approach, utilizing laurel leaves as the carbon precursor, o-phenylenediamine (OPD) as the nitrogen source, and N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC) as the solvent. Structural characterizations unveiled the presence of abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of G-CDs, significantly influencing their luminescent properties. The excitation and emission wavelengths of G-CDs were determined to be 467 nm and 508 nm, respectively, with a quantum yield of up to 49.9 %. Moreover, the emissions of G-CDs were found to be dependent on the excitation wavelength, suggesting a potential correlation between luminescence and particle size. Notably, the stability assessment demonstrated excellent salt resistance and strong photostability of the G-CDs. A brief discussion on the practical application of G-CDs in anti-counterfeit fluorescent ink highlighted their potential in this filed. This study presents a green synthesis strategy for producing high-performance carbon dots, reducing reliance on chemical reagents, lowering synthesis costs, and mitigating environmental pollution.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials that exhibit remarkable fluorescence properties [1,2]. They possess advantages such as low production cost, excellent water solubility, outstanding luminescent performance, and good biocompatibility [3–6]. In comparison to traditional organic or inorganic fluorophores, CDs also demonstrate exceptional resistance to photobleaching and photostability [7,8]. Even after several hours of exposure to xenon lamp irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of CDs remains nearly unchanged [9,10]. Furthermore, most CDs exhibit favorable fluorescence performance in high ionic strength or complex system [11,12]. Due to their excellent optical properties, CDs show tremendous potential applications in various fields, including fluorescence sensing [13–15], biological imaging [16–18], and analytical detection [19–21].

Currently, the primary methods for synthesizing CDs include hydrothermal or solvothermal method, microwave pyrolysis, and acid treatment, which involve the dehydration carbonization and polymerization of carbon-containing molecules such as citric acid, ethylenediamine, and urea [22–24]. In recent years, notable progress has been made in the synthesis, fluorescence properties, and applications of CDs. The raw materials for CDs can be chemical reagents or natural products. In fact, compared to chemical reagents, environmentally friendly natural products have more advantages and have become popular in the preparation of CDs. Natural products are renewable and exhibit good biocompatibility. Compared to conventional methods of synthesizing CDs using pure chemical reagents, the approach of utilizing natural products for CD synthesis is more environmentally friendly, enabling the conversion of low-value biomass waste into high-value products [25,26]. These advantages have attracted increasing attention to the study of CDs synthesis using natural biomass precursors. Extensive research has revealed that the optical properties of CDs are closely related to the precursor type, reaction medium, and synthesis conditions. Effective improvements in their optical properties can be achieved through elemental doping or surface state modification of CDs [27,28].

Laurus nobilis, native to the Mediterranean coast, was used by ancient Greeks to make laurel wreaths worn by victorious athletes. Laurel leaves (also known as bay leaves), the leaves of the Laurus nobilis, are popular spices used for seasoning or marinating food. The taste is aromatic, with an essential oil content of approximately 2 %, primarily composed of cineole (C10H18O) [29,30]. Using laurel leaves as the carbon precursor, we successfully synthesized high-performance green-emitting CDs (G-CDs) through a solvothermal method. Further investigation suggested that the G-CDs exhibited the best performance in ethanol (ETH), with a quantum yield of up to 49.9 %, surpassing many other green CDs. The stability of G-CDs was evaluated in terms of salt resistance and photostability, demonstrating excellent salt resistance and photostability of these CDs. This work has significant reference value for the synthesis of high quantum yield fluorescent CDs using natural products as raw materials and promotes the application of CDs.

Laurel leaves were purchased from Chengdu supermarket. O-phenylenediamine (OPD, analytical pure) and quinine sulfate (analytically pure) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd.). N,N-dimethylacetamide (DMAC), thiourea, nitric acid (HNO3,)ethanol (analytically pure) were purchased from Chengdu Kelon Chemical Co., Ltd.). The experimental water was self-made secondary distilled water.

The hydrothermal reaction vessel (25 mL) was purchased from Xi Niu Technology Co., Ltd. The ultrasonic cleaning machine was purchased from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd. The electric constant temperature blast drying oven was purchased from Shanghai Lichen Scientific and Technological Development Co., Ltd. All UV–vis spectra were measured by the Shunyu Hengping 756 PC UV–vis spectrophotometer, and fluorescence spectra were obtained by the Lengguang F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

G-CDs were synthesized by solvothermal method, where laurel leaves were used as the carbon precursor and DMAC as solvent. In details, 10 g laurel leaves and 200 mL ethanol were added to a blender to break and then soaked for 12 h. 5 mL laurel leaves extract, 1.2 g OPD, 0.5 g HNO3, and 5 mL DMAC were mixed, and sonicated for 10 min. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 5 h. Subsequently, the product solution was naturally cooled to room temperature and purified using a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) of 1000 Da for 48 h to obtain the G-CDs solution, which was then stored at 4 °C for future use. The preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 1.
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM): TEM and High resolution TEM images of G-CDs were collected with Tecnai G2 F20, (FEI Co., U.S.A.) operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FT-IR): the purified CDs solution was placed in a vacuum drying oven and thoroughly dried at 60 °C. The obtained CDs powder was then mixed with KBr at a ratio of 1:100, ground, and pressed into pellets, and then subjected to infrared testing with a scanning range of 4000–400 cm−1.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS): the dried G-CDs powder was prepared for surface composition using a Geneis 60 s X-ray photoelectron spectrometer, with C1s (284.6 eV) as the internal standard for binding energy correction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of relative fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
According to literature [31], the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution is 0.54 at 360 nm. Using quinine sulfate as a standard reference, the relative QY of sample can be estimated by the following formula:
(1)
Q
Y
U
= Q
Y
S
⋅
F
U
F
S
⋅
A
S
A
U
⋅
(
η
S
η
U
)
2
where F represents the integrated fluorescence intensity; A represents absorbance at excitation wavelength; η represents refractive index of solvent; Subscripts S and U refer to standard sample and test sample respectively. In order to avoid inner filter effect, absorbances of both standard and test samples should be less than 0.05.

After diluting the G-CDs solution with ethanol in a 1:1 ratio, it can be used as anti-counterfeiting fluorescent ink for writing or printing on white paper using fountain pens, stamps, and inkjet printers. The handwritting or drawing was photographed under sunlight and ultraviolet light respectively.

The morphology, structure and composition of G-CDs were analyzed using TEM, FT-IR, and XPS. As shown in Fig. 2a-c, these CDs particles are not welldispersed, with average particle sizes of about 3.8 nm. Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals well-defined lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.21 nm, attributed to the (100) lattice distance of the graphitic carbon [32]. The surface functional groups of CDs were characterized using FT-IR, as illustrated in Fig. 2d. The absorption peaks observed at 3700–3500 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of N-H or O-H bonds. The peak at 2385 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of OCO bonds. The peak at 1706 cm−1 arises from the stretching vibration of CC bonds. The peak at 1525 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration absorption peak of CN bonds. The peak at 1386 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration absorption peak of C-N bonds. The XPS analysis of G-CDs is depicted in Fig. 2e-h. The survey spectrum (Fig. 2e) exhibits characteristic peaks at 532.2 eV, 399.8 eV, and 285.0 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively. These findings suggest that G-CDs primarily consist of three elements: carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O), with atomic ratios of 76.09 %, 8.4 %, and 20.8 %, respectively. Further decomposition of the C1s peak reveals four distinct peaks at 283.8 eV, 284.8 eV, 285.6 eV, and 290.2 eV (Fig. 2f), which can be attributed to the functional groups of C-C/CC, C-N, C-O, and COOH, respectively. The peak observed at 532.2 eV in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) corresponds to the O-C bond. The peaks of N1s at 398.0 eV and 399.6 eV (Fig. 2h) indicate the presence of amino nitrogen and pyridine nitrogen. Combining the information from FT-IR, it can be inferred that the surface of G-CDs contains functional groups such as -OH, -C-N-, and -NH2.

UV–visible absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectroscopies were used to characterize the optical features of G-CDs. Fig. 3a presents a three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum of G-CDs, revealing a distinct excitation-emission peak. Fig. 3b depicts the corresponding two-dimensional projection, clearly demonstrating that the excitation region predominantly falls within the 300–500 nm range, while the emission region primarily spans from 450 nm to 600 nm. Fig. 3c showcases the UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of G-CDs. Notably, the UV–vis absorption spectrum exhibits a pronounced absorption band below 300 nm, indicative of typical aromatic benzene ring absorption. Furthermore, an absorption peak at 467 nm, in proximity to the excitation peak, signifies a characteristic absorption feature of G-CDs, originating mainly from the electronic transition of functional groups such as -CO or -NH2 on the surface of G-CDs. These absorbed photons are likely involved in the fluorescence emission process, suggesting that these functional groups may constitute part of the photoluminescent center (fluorophore). Additionally, the maximum excitation and emission peaks are observed at 467 nm and 508 nm, respectively. The area beneath the emission spectrum curve is colored to represent the fluorescence color corresponding to the emission wavelength. It can clear that the G-CDs exhibit a prominent green emission. In addition, the emission spectrum exhibits a Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of 74 nm.

The photoluminescence mechanism of G-CDs can be elucidated by investigating the excitation wavelength-dependent emission behavior. Herein, by gradient changing the excitation wavelength in the range of 410 nm∼510 nm, the corresponding emission spectra were recorded as shown in Fig. 4a. It can be seen that under different excitation wavelengths, the fluorescence intensity changes accordingly, and the emission wavelength also varies. For observation, these emission spectra are normalized, as shown in Fig. 4b, one can see that the emission wavelength has a significant red shift, reaching about 36 nm. This phenomenon indicates that the G-CDs exhibit excitation wavelength dependent emission behavior. It can be inferred that the G-CDs are excitation wavelength dependent nanomaterials, implying that the G-CDs have a typical core-shell structure, with graphene structured carbon nuclei inside and CO, -NH2 and other functional groups on the outer surface. The presence of these heteroatomic groups introduces structural defects at the edges of graphene, disrupting the conjugated structure of graphene. The luminescence behavior of the G-CDs is closely related to these surface defects [33,34] and effected by the particle size (conjugated structure) [35,36]. Therefore, within a certain range, changes in the excitation wavelength result in corresponding variations in the emission wavelength.

To investigate the influence of solvents on the luminescent properties of G-CDs, we measured their photoluminescence quantum yield (QY) in various solvents. Herein, water (WAT), methanol (MET), ethanol (ETH), acetone (ACE), tetrahydrofuran (THF), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were employed as solvents, and concentration dependent fluorescence and UV–vis spectra were recorded. The fitting curve of the integrated fluorescence intensity and absorbance is presented in Fig. 5a. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the QYs of G-CDs in different solvents were estimated based on the slopes of these fitting curves. The obtained QY values are displayed in Fig. 5b. The QY of G-CDs in different solvents were 13.2 % for water, 36.9 % for methanol, 49.9 % for ethanol, 28.3 % for acetone, 29.7 % for tetrahydrofuran, and 23.9 % for N,N-dimethylformamide. It is evident that the QY of G-CDs varies across different solvents, with the lowest QY observed in water and the highest QY in ethanol, indicating that the luminescence of CDs is significantly influenced by solvent environment. Moreover, G-CDs exhibit the highest QY in ethanol, reaching an impressive value of 49.9 %, surpassing that of most CDs synthesized using natural precursors, demonstrating significant application potential.

Chemical stability is crucial for the application of CDs in diverse environments. Firstly, we examined the stability of G-CDs in various solvents, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra are presented in Fig. 6a. To facilitate comparison, the maximum fluorescence intensity and maximum emission wavelength of G-CDs in each solvent were plotted in Fig. 6b and c, respectively. It is evident that G-CDs exhibit the highest fluorescence intensity in ethanol, while the lowest fluorescence intensity in water, in line with the QY observation. Moreover, G-CDs show the largest emission wavelength in water, while the smallest emission wavelength in acetone. Furthermore, we conducted additional investigations into the photostability of G-CDs. As shown in Fig. 6d, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs exhibited minimal changes even after continuous exposure to UV irradiation for 2 h, demonstrating remarkable resistance to photobleaching. Finally, we assessed the stability of G-CDs in NaCl solutions with varying concentrations. As illustrated in Fig. 6e, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs remained constant within the range of 0–500 mM NaCl concentration, highlighting their robust tolerance to salt.

Fluorescent CDs are a kind of promising fluorescent materials, which can be used in the fields of analytical detection, biological imaging, energy catalysis and optoelectronic devices, etc. Herein, serving as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink is taken as an example to briefly introduce the application of G-CDs. After diluting the as-prepared G-CDs solution with ethanol in a 1:1 ratio, it can be used as UV encrypted fluorescent ink directly. Fig. 7 displays a classical chinese poetry written with the G-CDs as fluorescent ink. Under sunlight, it looks like a blank piece of white paper, as shown in Fig. 7a. Under a 395 nm UV lamp, the poem is clearly visible, as shown in Fig. 7b. The G-CDs solution could be used as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink for pen, brush, ink-jet printer, seal, etc.

In this study, high-performance G-CDs were successfully synthesized through a one-step solvothermal synthesis using laurel leaves as the carbon source, o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source, and DMAC as the solvent. The excitation and emission wavelengths of G-CDs were 467 nm and 508 nm, respectively. Structural characterizations unveiled the presence of abundant nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface of G-CDs. Moreover, the emission behavior of the G-CDs was found to be dependent on the excitation wavelength, suggesting a potential correlation between the luminescence and the particle size of the G-CDs. Furthermore, the luminescence of the G-CDs was assessed in various solvents, and G-CDs exhibited the highest performance in ethanol, with a photoluminescence quantum yield of up to 49.9 %, surpassing that of most other G-CDs. Additionally, the G-CDs demonstrated excellent salt tolerance and resistance to photobleaching. Finally, the application of the G-CDs was briefly introduced, highlighting their potential use in anti-counterfeit ink. This study presents an important strategy for developing high-performance, long-wavelength fluorescent CDs using natural products as precursors.

Xiang Long: Investigation, Synthetic Chemistry, Spectral acquisition, Writing – original draft. Jiayang Wang: Conceptualization, Discussion, Review. Yanshun Ma: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Investigation. Shaogui Wu: Supervision, Investigation, computation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots: advances in nanocarbon applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface modification and chemical functionalization of carbon dots: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Yan, Y. Jiang, X. Sun, Z. Bai, Y. Zhang, X. Zhou, Surface modification and chemical functionalization of carbon dots: a review, Microchimica Acta, 185 (2018) 1–34.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaluation of antifungal and anti-ochratoxigenic activities of Salvia officinalis, Lavandula dentata and Laurus nobilis essential oils and a major monoterpene constituent 1, 8-cineole against Aspergillus carbonarius

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I. Dammak, Z. Hamdi, S.K. El Euch, H. Zemni, A. Mliki, M. Hassouna, S. Lasram, Evaluation of antifungal and anti-ochratoxigenic activities of Salvia officinalis, Lavandula dentata and Laurus nobilis essential oils and a major monoterpene constituent 1, 8-cineole against Aspergillus carbonarius, Industrial Crops and Products, 128 (2019) 85–93.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Priming effects of leaves of Laurus nobilis L. and 1, 8-cineole on carbon mineralization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Kocak, C. Darici, Priming effects of leaves of Laurus nobilis L. and 1, 8-cineole on carbon mineralization, Chilean journal of agricultural research, 76 (2016) 100–104.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for detection of lead, cadmium, and mercury ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H.N. Kim, W.X. Ren, J.S. Kim, J. Yoon, Fluorescent and colorimetric sensors for detection of lead, cadmium, and mercury ions, Chemical Society Reviews, 41 (2012) 3210–3244.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green and blue aggregation-induced emissive carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xu, L. Mo, W. Li, Y. Li, B. Lei, X. Zhang, J. Zhuang, C. Hu, Y. Liu, Red, green and blue aggregation-induced emissive carbon dots, Chinese Chemical Letters, 32 (2021) 3927–3930.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon nanodots: mechanisms of photoluminescence and principles of application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: I.Y. Goryacheva, A.V. Sapelkin, G.B. Sukhorukov, Carbon nanodots: mechanisms of photoluminescence and principles of application, TrAC Trends in Analytical Chemistry, 90 (2017) 27–37.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Excitation-independent carbon dots, from photoluminescence mechanism to single-color application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.-H. Wen, X.-B. Yin, Excitation-independent carbon dots, from photoluminescence mechanism to single-color application, RSC advances, 6 (2016) 27829–27835.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-trapped exciton emission from carbon dots investigated by polarization anisotropy of photoluminescence and photoexcitation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Xiao, Y. Wang, Y. Huang, T. Wong, H. Sun, Self-trapped exciton emission from carbon dots investigated by polarization anisotropy of photoluminescence and photoexcitation, Nanoscale, 9 (2017) 12637–12646.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Theoretical understanding of structure–property relationships in luminescence of carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yu, X. Yong, Z. Tang, B. Yang, S. Lu, Theoretical understanding of structure–property relationships in luminescence of carbon dots, The Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters, 12 (2021) 7671–7687.
